messages.content: {"success": true, "name": "nextjs-deployment-debugging", "description": "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing.", "tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"], "content": "--- name: nextjs-deployment-debugging description: "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing." version: 1.0.0 author: Hermes Agent license: MIT metadata: hermes: tags: [nextjs, deployment, docker, debugging, build, routes, pages-router, app-router] related_skills: [systematic-debugging, ecommerce-codebase-audit, kanban-orchestrator] --- # Next.js Deployment & Debugging ## Overview This skill covers debugging and fixing Next.js deployment issues: build failures, routes not registering, standalone output problems, Docker build issues with GitHub Packages, and hybrid App Router / Pages Router troubleshooting. ### `pages/` and `app/` Directory Conflict When switching from App Router to Pages Router, a **stale root-level `app/` directory** (not `src/app/`) will cause: ``` pages and app directories should be under the same folder ``` **Fix:** Remove the stale `app/` directory at the repo root: ```bash rm -rf app ``` Always check for both `src/app/` AND `app/` when cleaning up. ## Working Patterns for Section-Based Content Sites When building a site that serves JSON-driven sectioned content from `getServerSideProps`, use this architecture: ### File structure ``` src/pages/index.tsx ← home page (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/[slug].tsx ← all other pages (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/admin/content.tsx ← admin editor (static, uses client-kit) src/pages/_document.tsx ← custom HTML shell src/pages/globals.css ← tailwind-based theme (not _app.tsx styling) src/components/sections.tsx ← all visual section components src/components/Header.tsx ← navigation bar src/components/Footer.tsx ← footer src/components/content.ts ← content resolver helper ``` ### Component section mapping pattern Each section in the page config JSON (e.g. `{id:"hero",variant:"split",content:"home.hero"}`) maps to a component via a `SECTION_MAP` record: ```tsx const SECTION_MAP: Record = { 'hero': HeroSection, 'stats-counter': StatsSection, 'trust-signals': TrustSection, 'programs-comparison': ProgramsSection, 'services': ServicesSection, 'tax-savings-calculator': TaxCalculatorSection, 'why-destination': WhyCountrySection, 'features': FeaturesSection, 'process-timeline': ProcessSection, 'testimonials': TestimonialsSection, 'cta-banner': CtaBanner, } ``` ### Data flow: pageContent shape MUST match component keys **CRITICAL:** The `pageContent` object passed to each component must have keys matching what the component looks up. The standard pattern: ```tsx // In the dispatch (pages/[slug].tsx or pages/index.tsx): const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id) const pc: any = {} const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id // "home.hero" → "hero" if (section.content || section.id) pc[keyName] = sectionData || content return ``` This creates `{hero: {...}}` from section.content=`"home.hero"`, matching what `HeroSection` expects (`pageContent.hero`). **Common failure modes:** 1. **section.id used as key instead of section.content suffix:** If the dispatch uses `pc[section.id]` (e.g. `pc["programs-comparison"]`) but the component looks for `pageContent.programs`, the data is stored under a key the component can't find. Always extract the last segment of the `section.content` dotpath. 2. **pageContent is the raw sectionData, not wrapped:** If you pass `pageContent = sectionData` directly (the resolved object), then `HeroSection` does `pageContent.hero` which is `undefined` because `pageContent` IS the hero object. The old `buildPageContent()` pattern wrapped it as `{hero: {...}}`. 3. **Components reading `pageContent` directly instead of via `.KEY`:** New components (in sections-extra.tsx) use `const d = data || pageContent || {}` where `data` is the raw section data passed as a separate prop. This is the preferred pattern. Older components (in sections.tsx) use `const c = pageContent.KEY || {}` which relies on the wrapping. Both work as long as the dispatch creates the correct shape. ### Brand tokens (CSS custom properties) Always apply brand colors as inline styles or CSS variables — Tailwind `@theme` directives in `globals.css` are ignored by Pages Router: ```css /* src/pages/globals.css */ @import "tailwindcss"; @theme { --color-primary: #1B2A4A; --color-secondary: #C9A96E; --color-accent: #C9A96E; --color-background: #FFFFFF; --color-surface: #FFFFFF; --color-surface-alt: #F5F5F0; --color-text: #1B2A4A; --color-text-muted: #5C6B7A; --color-border: #E0E0E0; --color-success: #10b981; } ``` Then in components, reference inline: `#1B2A4A` for navy, `#C9A96E` for gold, `#F5F5F0` for light backgrounds. ### SEO per page For pages, load SEO data from `content.es.json` at the matching page key path: ```tsx const seo = resolveContent(content, `${pageId}Page.seo`) || resolveContent(content, `${pageId}.seo`) || pageConfig?.seoTitle || {} ``` Set `` and `<meta name="description">` from this data. ### Content shape reference for section components The `content/es.json` in this project has this nesting: ```json { "siteName": "...", "tagline": "...", "navigation": { "navItems": [...], "ctaText": "...", "ctaHref": "..." }, "home": { "hero": { "headline": "...", "subheadline": "...", "ctaPrimaryText": "...", "ctaPrimaryHref": "...", "ctaSecondaryText": "...", "trustBadges": [...] }, "stats": { "items": [{ "value": "...", "label": "...", "icon": "..." }] }, "trust": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "icon": "...", "title": "...", "description": "...", "image": "..." }] }, "programs": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "tiers": [{ "id": "...", "name": "...", "price": "...", "included": [...], "highlighted": bool }] }, "services": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "groups": [{ "title": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] }] }, "whyCountry": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "pillars": [{ "title": "...", "description": "...", "bullets": [...] }] }, "process": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "steps": [{ "number": int, "title": "...", "description": "...", "duration": "..." }] }, "testimonials": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "author": "...", "role": "...", "quote": "...", "rating": int }] }, "finalCta": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "buttonText": "...", "buttonHref": "..." }, "taxCalculator": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "..." }, "beneluxDesk": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] } }, "footer": { "businessName": "...", "columns": [{ "title": "...", "links": [{ "label": "...", "href": "..." }] }], "copyright": "..." }, "whatsapp": "595...", "contactPage": { "hero": {...}, "contact": {...} }, "faqPage": { "full": { "items": [{ "q": "...", "a": "..." }] } }, "aboutPage": { "hero": {...}, "story": { "paragraphs": [...] }, "team": { "members": [{ "name": "...", "role": "..." }] }, "gallery": { "images": [{"$img":"..."}] } }, // ... 20+ more page content entries } ``` ### Brand tokens (from tokens.json) ```json { "primary": "#1B2A4A", // navy — headers, backgrounds, buttons "secondary": "#C9A96E", // gold — CTAs, accents, highlights "accent": "#C9A96E", "background": "#FFFFFF", "surface": "#FFFFFF", "surfaceLight": "#EDE8DB", // caramel tint "text": "#1B2A4A", "textLight": "#333333", "textMuted": "#6B6B6B" } ``` ## Critical Knowledge: Next.js 16 Turbopack Bug **Next.js 16.2.4 with Turbopack has a confirmed bug where root App Router pages are silently dropped from the route table.** The following patterns produce NO route output: - `src/app/page.tsx` — root page is compiled (shows 4/4 in build) but produces NO `page.js` file and is NOT in routes-manifest - `src/app/[[...slug]]/page.tsx` — catch-all routes are completely ignored by Turbopack - `src/app/index/page.tsx` — same behavior, no route registered - `src/app/home/page.tsx` — same behavior **Only these routes work in App Router mode:** - `/_not-found` (handled internally) - `/_global-error` (handled internally) - Any explicit named route like `/admin/content` (works fine) - API routes like `/api/admin/content` (work fine) **Root cause:** Turbopack in Next.js 16 silently drops root-level pages that use synchronous filesystem I/O (`readFileSync`, `readdirSync`) or async patterns — even trivial `<h1>Hello</h1>` pages. The bug is in Turbopack's route discovery, not the build output. ## Workaround: Switch to Pages Router When App Router root pages fail to register: 1. **Delete `src/app/`** entirely (App Router and Pages Router can't coexist in Next.js 16) 2. **Create `src/pages/index.tsx`** using `getServerSideProps` (Pages Router SSR): ```tsx // src/pages/index.tsx import { readFileSync } from 'fs' import path from 'path' export default function Home({ content }: any) { return <main>...</main> } export function getServerSideProps() { const data = JSON.parse(readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), 'content', 'es.json'), 'utf-8')) return { props: { content: data } } } ``` 3. **Move other App Router pages** to Pages Router: - `src/app/admin/content/page.tsx` → `src/pages/admin/content.tsx` (static or dynamic) - `src/app/api/admin/content/route.ts` → N/A — Pages Router uses API routes differently 4. **Convert `next.config.ts` to `next.config.js`** — Pages-only mode doesn't support .ts config files ### Build output for Pages Router (working) ``` Route (pages) ┌ ƒ / ← Dynamic SSR page ├ ○ /404 ← Static error page ├ ○ /admin/content ← Static admin page ƒ Proxy (Middleware) ← Request logging ``` ## Docker Build with GitHub Packages ### Issue: `npm ci` fails with lock file mismatch When `package-lock.json` has `@ai-whisperers/*` packages but `package.json` doesn't specify versions, `npm ci` errors: ``` Invalid: lock file's @ai-whisperers/admin@0.2.1 does not satisfy @ai-whisperers/admin@ ``` **Fix:** Use `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` instead of `npm ci` in the Dockerfile. ### Issue: npm install fails inside Docker for GitHub Packages ``` npm error 404 Not Found - GET https://registry.npmjs.org/@ai-whisperers%2fclient-kit - Not found ``` **Fix:** The Dockerfile's deps stage must COPY `.npmrc`: ```dockerfile COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` Without `.npmrc`, the Docker build can't reach `npm.pkg.github.com`. ### Issue: Content/static files missing in Docker image Even with `COPY --from=builder` in the Dockerfile, `content/` and `nexa-pages/` directories may be missing in the runner. **The `npm ci` failure causes the builder stage to fail silently** (Docker caches the previous successful build, so `COPY` from the failed builder stage is skipped, reverting to the cached runner stage). **Fix:** Always verify content files exist in the final image: ```bash docker run --rm my-image:prod ls -la /app/content ``` ## Tests for Content/Data Loading in SSR ### Live content audit script When a site serves HTTP 200 but only shows `<h1>` with no actual sections, create a standalone audit script: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import json, urllib.request # Check content resolution c = json.load(open('content/es.json')) print('Content keys:', list(c.keys())) print('Home keys:', list(c['home'].keys())) # Check page config sections p = json.load(open('nexa-pages/home.json')) for s in p['sections']: key = s.get('content') or s.get('id') sc = c.get(key) print(f' Section {s[\"id\"]}: key={key} direct={!!sc}') if not sc and key and '.' in key: sc = key.split('.').reduce(lambda o,k: o.get(k) if o else None, c) print(f' dot-path={!!sc}') # Check live site h = urllib.request.urlopen('https://example.com/').read().decode() print(f'Size: {len(h)}') print(f'Has hero: {\"headline\" in h}') ``` ### Dot-notation content resolution pattern Content in `content/es.json` is nested (e.g. `c.home.hero.headline`), but section configs use dot-notation strings like `"home.hero"`. The SSR component must resolve via path traversal: ```tsx // WRONG — returns null because "home.hero" is not a flat key let sc = content?.["home.hero"]; // RIGHT — traverses home → hero → object let sc = "home.hero".split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content); ``` Always include this helper in Pages Router SSR components: ```tsx function resolveContent(content: any, key: string): any { if (!content || !key) return null let sc = content[key] if (!sc && key.includes('.')) { sc = key.split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content) } return sc } ``` ### Testing HTTP from inside a container BusyBox `wget` in Alpine containers can return `Connection refused` even when the HTTP server IS listening. Use Node.js TCP socket instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const http = require('http'); http.get({hostname:'localhost',port:3000,path:'/'}, r => { let d = ''; r.on('data', c => d += c); r.on('end', () => console.log(r.statusCode, d.length, 'bytes')); }).on('error', e => console.log('ERR:', e.message)); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 3000); " ``` ## File Corruption: `patch` Tool + read_file View Pitfall **The `patch` tool writes the raw file content from the conversation context.** If the file was previously read with `read_file` (which formats output with line-number prefixes like ` 1|{`), those prefixes ARE NOT in the file on disk — they're display formatting injected by the tool. But if you use `execute_code` with a `write_file()` call that passes the read_file output string directly, the line-number prefixes get written into the file, corrupting it. **Symptoms:** - `JSONDecodeError: Extra data: line 1 column 7 (char 6)` — the `1|` prefix makes JSON invalid - Git shows `1|{` as the first line of the file - The file appears in `git log` with `+501 -501` or similar large diffs **How it happens:** ```python # WRONG — read_file's display format includes line numbers content = read_file("images.json") # Returns {"content": " 1|{\n 2| ..."} write_file("images.json", content["content"]) # Writes the line-number prefix! ``` **Fix:** 1. Restore from git: `git checkout -- <file>` 2. Or restore from a known-good source copy 3. Re-apply edits using `patch` (which operates on the actual file on disk, not the display format) **Best practice for editing JSON files:** - Use `patch` for surgical replacements (it modifies the file on disk directly) - Use Python scripts with `json.load` / `json.dump` for bulk transformations - NEVER pipe read_file output back into write_file **Verification after editing:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file.json')); print('Valid ✓')" ``` ## Standalone Repo Divergence: Content Path Fixes When a standalone site repo is forked from a multi-tenant builder (e.g. `paragu-ai-builder` → `nexa-paraguay`), the content JSON files retain multi-tenant paths that break in standalone mode. ### Paths that must be fixed | Multi-tenant path | Standalone fix | |---|---| | `/s/es/nexa-paraguay/programas` | `/programas` | | `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/contacto` | `/contacto` | | `/s/de/nexa-paraguay/benelux` | `/benelux` | | `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/logo.webp` | `/images/logo.webp` | ### Where these paths hide 1. **Navigation hrefs** — `navigation.navItems[].href` — every nav link 2. **CTA buttons** — `home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref`, `home.finalCta.buttonHref` 3. **Program tier CTAs** — `home.programs.tiers[].ctaHref` 4. **Footer links** — `footer.columns[].links[].href` 5. **Image manifests** — `images.json` → `basePath`, every `src` and `fallbackSrc` 6. **Testimonials** — `testimonials.json` → `image`, `videoPoster` 7. **Content file image references** — only if they use `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/` directly ### How to search ```bash grep -rn "/s/es/nexa-paraguay/" content/ # locale paths grep -rn "/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/" content/ images.json # image paths ``` Count them: ```bash grep -c "/s/es/nexa-paraguay" content/es.json # should be 0 in standalone ``` ### Dockerfile missing runtime data files When a Next.js Pages Router site loads data from `getServerSideProps` via `readFileSync('images.json')`, the Dockerfile's runtime stage MUST include that file: ```dockerfile # WRONG — images.json not copied, runtime returns null for ALL image refs COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content # RIGHT — all runtime data files included COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json # ← REQUIRED COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages ``` **Symptom of missing data files:** Site loads with HTTP 200, has correct header/footer/nav, but `<img>` tags resolve to empty alt-only elements. No error in logs because `loadJSON` returns `{}` instead of throwing. **Verification:** Check inside the running container: ```bash docker exec $CONTAINER ls -la /app/images.json # If "No such file or directory", the file wasn't copied in Dockerfile ``` ### Pages Router: If all pages show HTTP 200 but section content is missing Check three things: 1. **Page config has the right `sections` array** — each section needs `id` and `content` fields referencing paths that exist in `content/es.json` 2. **SLUG_MAP has an entry** — every `nexa-pages/*.json` file needs a corresponding entry in `SLUG_MAP` in `[slug].tsx`. If the slug isn't in the map, the page returns 404. 3. **Rich fallback renderer** — the generic section renderer in `[slug].tsx` must handle more than just `headline`/`subheadline`. It should also render: - `eyebrow` — small uppercase label - `body` or `content` — plain text paragraphs split by `\n` - `data.items`, `data.groups`, `data.pillars`, `data.full.items` — arrays rendered as card grids - Items support: `title/pregunta/question/term` + `description/respuesta/answer/definition/body` `docker stack deploy` can silently keep the old container running if the image tag hasn't changed. Docker doesn't re-pull or rebuild by tag; it only creates a new task when the digest changes. **Symptom:** You pushed code, rebuilt the Docker image, redeployed, but the old content still shows. **Fix: Force update the service** ```bash docker service update --force <service_name> ``` **Verify the new container is running:** ```bash docker service ps <service_name> # Check CURRENT STATE shows "Running X seconds ago", not "Running X hours ago" ``` **Verify content changes took effect:** ```bash curl -s https://site.com/ | grep "expected string" ``` Or check inside the container: ```bash docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<service_name> -q) cat /app/content/es.json | head -5 ``` ## Testing Build Output ### Check which routes Next.js registered ```bash # Routes manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/routes-manifest.json')); print([r['page'] for r in d.get('staticRoutes',[])])" # Prerender manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/prerender-manifest.json')); print(list(d['routes'].keys()))" # Build output shows "Route (app)" or "Route (pages)" section ``` ### Check compiled page files ```bash find .next -name 'page.js' -type f | sort ``` If root pages are missing (no `page.js` for `/`), suspect the Turbopack bug. ### Test standalone server ```bash cd .next/standalone && PORT=3000 HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 node server.js & sleep 5 && curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/ ``` #### Healthcheck Pitfalls Next.js standalone startup prints "Ready in 0ms" BEFORE the HTTP server actually calls `accept()`. A healthcheck immediately after startup will cause the container to restart in a loop. **Don't use a healthcheck or use a generous start period:** ```yaml healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:3000/"] start_period: 30s interval: 30s ``` **Route selection for healthchecks:** Must ping a URL returning HTTP 200. Test locally first: ```bash docker run --rm -d -p 13000:3000 <image> && sleep 5 && curl -I http://localhost:13000/<route> ``` Common pitfalls: - Root `/` may return 404 with Next 16 Turbopack route group bug (`(store)/page.tsx`) - `/admin/content` is a good target if the admin panel loads - `/api/health` is ideal if the app exposes one - BusyBox `wget` returns `Connection refused` on Alpine even when ready BusyBox `wget` (Alpine) may get `Connection refused` even when the socket is ready. Use `node -e` to test HTTP internally instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const net = require('net'); const c = net.createConnection(3000, '127.0.0.1'); c.on('connect', () => { c.write('GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n'); }); c.on('data', d => console.log(d.toString().split('\r\n')[0])); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 5000); " ``` ## GitHub Packages + Docker Build: Auth Workaround ### Issue: Docker build can't authenticate to npm.pkg.github.com When building a Next.js site with `@ai-whisperers/*` private packages, Docker build fails at `npm install` because it lacks a `GITHUB_TOKEN`: ``` npm error 401 Unauthorized - GET https://npm.pkg.github.com/@ai-whisperers%2fseo ``` Also: even when using `--build-arg GITHUB_TOKEN`, Docker's BuildKit security scanner blocks `ENV GITHUB_TOKEN` in the Dockerfile with: ``` SecretsUsedInArgOrEnv: Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data ``` This means standard `docker build --build-arg` + `ENV` approach won't work with modern Docker. ### Solution A: Prebuild on host, use lightweight Docker image (RECOMMENDED) The `.npmrc` file reads `$GITHUB_TOKEN` from the environment, but Docker build can't access the host's environment. Instead: 1. Run `npm install && npm run build` on the host (VPS) where `GITHUB_TOKEN` is available 2. Use a separate production Dockerfile (`Dockerfile.prod`) that ONLY copies the already-built output — no npm install needed: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — use AFTER building on the host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=10s --retries=3 CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://127.0.0.1:3000/ || exit 1 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Important:** This Dockerfile uses `node_modules/.bin/next start` not the `server.js` from standalone mode, because the host build produces a `.next` directory suitable for `next start`. Deploy sequence: ```bash cd /root/pitchy-website git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npx next build docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t pitchy:prod . docker service update pitchy_web --force --image pitchy:prod ``` ### Solution B: Docker build-time secret (for CI/CD) In GitHub Actions, pass the token as a Docker build secret: ```yaml - name: Build Docker run: | docker build \ --secret id=GITHUB_TOKEN,env=GITHUB_TOKEN \ -t mysite:prod . ``` Dockerfile: ```dockerfile RUN --mount=type=secret,id=GITHUB_TOKEN \ GITHUB_TOKEN=$(cat /run/secrets/GITHUB_TOKEN) npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ## SvelteKit Static Site Deployment (nginx + adapter-static) When deploying a SvelteKit site with `@sveltejs/adapter-static` behind nginx/Docker, the workflow differs from Next.js. ### Dockerfile (nginx alpine) ```dockerfile FROM nginx:alpine COPY build /usr/share/nginx/html COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf ``` ### nginx.conf (SPA-friendly) ```nginx server { listen 80; server_name _; root /usr/share/nginx/html; index index.html; location / { try_files $uri $uri.html $uri/ =404; } location /_app/ { expires 1y; add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable"; } } ``` **Note:** `try_files $uri $uri.html` is critical — SvelteKit prerenders `/gallery` as `gallery.html`, so nginx must serve `$uri.html` as a fallback. ### Common Pitfall: Missing prerender entries **Symptom:** Page builds fine locally (`.svelte-kit/` has the route) but 404s in production. **Root cause 1 — prerender entries list:** The `svelte.config.js` `prerender.entries` array must explicitly list every route: ```js kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { entries: ['/', '/about', '/gallery', '/tickets', '/hotel', '/schedule', '/faq', '/rules', '/contact', '/login', '/privacy', '/dealers-den', '/robots.txt', '/sitemap.xml'], handleUnseenRoutes: 'ignore' } } ``` **Root cause 2 — missing `+page.ts`:** Even with the correct entries list, a route won't prerender without a `+page.ts` file exporting `prerender = true`: ```typescript // src/routes/gallery/+page.ts export const prerender = true; ``` **Verification:** Check `build/` directory for the page: ```bash ls build/gallery.html # must exist ``` ### Cross-Page Navigation Pattern for Multi-Page Sites For 10+ page SvelteKit static sites, ensure every page is reachable: 1. **Navbar** — list ALL pages (not just a subset). For anthro-party-argentina: Inicio, Entradas, Hotel, Cronograma, Dealers Den, Galería, Sobre APA!, FAQ, Reglamento, Contacto, Inscribite 2. **Footer** — organized columns by category (Evento, Información, Redes). Include sitemap.xml link. 3. **Cross-page links** at bottom of each page — 3 related pages per page with arrow indicators. For example: Tickets page links to Cronograma, Hotel, FAQ. Schedule links to Entradas, Dealers Den, Hotel. 4. **All images must have descriptive `alt` text** for accessibility. ### Svelte svelte.config.js ```js import adapter from '@sveltejs/adapter-static'; const config = { kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { origin: 'https://yourdomain.com', entries: ['/', ...allRoutes] } } }; ``` **Pitfall:** The `origin` in prerender config fixes the canonical URL leak (`http://sveltekit-prerender`). Without it, search engines see the build hostname. ## Docker Compose + Traefik: Label Placement Pitfall **The MOST COMMON cause of "works locally, 404 online"** after a Next.js fix is **Traefik labels not being applied to the Docker container**. ### The Bug In `docker-compose.yml`, labels under `deploy:` are **ignored by `docker compose up -d`** — they are only read by `docker stack deploy` (Swarm mode): ```yaml services: web: labels: # CORRECT — service-level labels work with both - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.rule=Host(`example.com`)" deploy: labels: # WRONG — ignored by `docker compose up -d` - "traefik.enable=true" ``` ### How to verify labels are applied ```bash docker inspect $CONTAINER_ID --format '{{json .Config.Labels}}' | python3 -c ' import sys,json; l=json.load(sys.stdin) for k,v in sorted(l.items()): if "traefik" in k.lower(): print(k,"=",v) ' ``` If **only** `com.docker.compose.*` labels appear, Traefik labels weren't applied. ### The Fix 1. Move labels from `deploy:` to the service level in docker-compose.yml 2. Use `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml $STACK_NAME` instead of `docker compose up -d` 3. Verify: `[REDACTED_DOCKER_CMD]` should show the service with labels applied ### CI/CD Secrets Access: Standalone vs Reusable Workflows When using GitHub Actions reusable workflows from `Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd`, the called workflow may NOT have access to the calling repo's org-level secrets (VPS_HOST, VPS_KEY, etc.), even with `secrets: inherit`. **Symptom:** CI run shows "This run likely failed because of a workflow file issue" with 0 jobs. **Fix:** Use a self-contained deploy workflow that doesn't depend on the `ci-cd` repo: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest timeout-minutes: 20 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - name: Install dependencies run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - name: Build run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/mysite && \ git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=\$(gh auth token) && \ npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npx next build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t mysite:prod . && \ docker service update mysite_web --force --image mysite:prod" ``` Alternatively, deploy directly from the agent via SSH (`ssh root@VPS ...`) which is faster and doesn't depend on GitHub Actions secrets at all. ```bash # Test from inside Traefik container (on same network as service) docker exec $TRAEFIK_CONTAINER sh -c 'wget -q -O - --timeout=5 http://$SERVICE_NAME:3000/ 2>&1 | head -3' # Test via Traefik directly (bypassing Cloudflare) curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' http://localhost:80/ curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' --insecure https://localhost/ ``` ### Traffic flow diagram ``` Browser → Cloudflare (orange) → VPS:443 → Traefik → agent-net → container:3000 → Next.js ``` 404 at any point in this chain breaks the site. ### SSR Content Duplication: Empty Container Shell When investigating a suspected duplicate content section, **check whether the second "section" is actually an empty component** with no visible content rather than a true content duplicate. The CTA banner section (`cta-banner`) is the most common culprit — it renders a `style="padding:2rem 1rem;background:#F5F5F0"` container shell even when the content data resolves to `null` or empty. **Fix:** Add a null-guard in the section dispatch or the component itself: ```tsx // In the dispatch loop ([[...slug]].tsx): {sections.map((section, idx) => { const Component = SECTION_MAP[section.id] const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content) if (!sectionData || Object.keys(sectionData).length === 0) return null // ... render })} ``` For reference material on this debugging path, see `references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md`. ## PPR + 'use client' + dynamic(): Content in RSC Payload But Empty `<main>` **Symptom:** Page loads with HTTP 200. Header, footer, and nav render correctly. `<main></main>` is empty in the HTML. The full content JSON is present in `self.__next_f.push` RSC script tags (visible via curl). Content appears when JavaScript fully hydrates. **Root cause chain:** 1. The `SectionsRenderer` component is marked `'use client'` at the top of the file 2. Each section component is loaded via `next/dynamic` with `{ ssr: true }` 3. With PPR (`cacheComponents: true` in next.config.js), the route is `◐ Partial Prerender` 4. PPR builds a static HTML shell at build-time that contains an empty `<main>` container 5. The dynamic content streams via RSC payload into the script tags, then React hydrates and fills `<main>` on the client **This is NOT a bug — it's how Next.js App Router + PPR works.** The content renders correctly in the browser. But if the user checks the HTML source directly (curl, View Source), they see empty `<main>`. ### When to fix it Fix when: - Users check the page source and think the page is broken - SEO crawlers don't render JavaScript (Google usually does render JS, but no guarantee) - Slow connections where the RSC payload takes time to download and hydrate ### Fixes (in order of preference) **Option 1: Disable PPR entirely** (fastest, safest) ```js // next.config.js — remove cacheComponents: true const nextConfig = { // cacheComponents: true, ← DELETE THIS output: "standalone", } ``` Then remove any `<Suspense>` wrappers from page components: ```tsx // Before: <Suspense><SectionsRenderer ... /></Suspense> // After: <SectionsRenderer ... /> ``` All routes become `● SSG` (fully pre-rendered HTML). **Option 2: Remove 'use client' from SectionsRenderer** (more work, better SSR) Convert SectionsRenderer to a Server Component. Import section components directly instead of via `dynamic()`: ```tsx // Instead of: import dynamic from 'next/dynamic' const HeroSection = dynamic(() => import('./sections').then(m => ({ default: m.HeroSection })), { ssr: true }) // Use: import { HeroSection } from './sections' ``` **Caveat:** This breaks if the section components import `'use client'` features (hooks, browser APIs). Sections that need client interactivity must stay as client components. **Option 3: Accept the current behavior** (valid if you control the SEO narrative) Content is in the RSC payload and renders via JS hydration. Real browsers see it. The `<main>` being empty in raw HTML is an architectural choice, not a bug. ### Content Resolution for sections.tsx Components When sections use the `content: "home"` pattern in page configs: ```json // nexa-pages/home.json { "id": "hero", "content": "home" } { "id": "trust-signals", "content": "home" } ``` The `resolveContent(content, "home")` returns the `content.home` object. Components like `HeroSection` do `pageContent.hero` — they expect `pageContent` to be the `home` object so they can access sub-keys. **Fix when a section returns null despite content existing:** 1. Check what key the component accesses: `pageContent.hero`, `const d = data || pageContent`, `d.groups` 2. Ensure the `content` key resolves to the right depth: - `content: "home"` → for components that access sub-keys (`pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.trust`) - `content: "home.feedback"` → for components that use the resolved data directly (`data.title`) 3. For sections using `data || pageContent || {}` with `d.groups`, check if the data has `groups` at the expected path: ```tsx // Fix: add fallback path const groups = d.services?.groups || d.groups || [] ``` **Verification:** After any content key fix, rebuild and check: ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -c "expected headline text" ``` ### Dynamic Import in Server Component Error If you import directly in a Server Component and hit `Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null`: - The section files likely have `'use client'` at the top - `dynamic()` is the correct loading pattern for client components in Server Components - When removing PPR, keep `dynamic()` with `{ ssr: true }` — it still renders server-side HTML but handles the client boundary correctly - Only exit-popup type components need `{ ssr: false }` ## Troubleshooting Checklist When a Next.js site shows 404 or doesn't serve content: 1. [ ] Check build output — are the expected routes listed? 2. [ ] Check `routes-manifest.json` — are routes registered? 3. [ ] Check `page.js` files — are they compiled? 4. [ ] Test HTTP inside container — `node -e` with raw TCP connection 5. [ ] Check `content/` and page configuration files exist in image 6. [ ] For GitHub Packages: check `.npmrc` was copied to Docker build cache 7. [ ] For standalone: check server.js runs without EADDRINUSE 8. [ ] Verify Traefik labels point to correct service port (3000) 9. [ ] Verify Traefik labels use correct Docker network 10. [ ] Check Cloudflare is proxying to VPS origin (not cached page) ", "path": "software-development/nextjs-deployment-debugging/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/software-development/nextjs-deployment-debugging", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md", "references/nexa-ppr-content-debugging.md", "references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md", "references/private-npm-docker-workflow.md", "references/component-architecture-pattern.md", "references/nexa-debugging-transcript.md", "references/nextjs-section-content-pipeline.md"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available", "metadata": {"hermes": {"tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"]}}}

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "nextjs-content-audit", "description": "Audit and fix Next.js content-driven sites where JSON content exists but doesn't render. Covers page config → section component mapping, generic fallback renderers, data key standardization, theming, and architectural cleanup for content-heavy Next.js Pages Router sites.", "tags": ["nextjs", "content", "audit", "architecture", "section-components", "theming", "standardization"], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: nextjs-content-audit
description: "Audit and fix Next.js content-driven sites where JSON content exists but doesn't render. Covers page config → section component mapping, generic fallback renderers, data key standardization, theming, and architectural cleanup for content-heavy Next.js Pages Router sites."
version: 1.0.0
tags: [nextjs, content, audit, architecture, section-components, theming, standardization]
---

# Next.js Content Audit & Standardization

## When to Use

A content-heavy Next.js site where:
- JSON content files exist (content/es.json, images.json) but pages show empty shells
- Generic fallback renderer shows only headings, ignores items/paragraphs/members/pillars
- 25+ page configs reference sections with no matching component
- Data key names vary across pages (q/a vs pregunta/respuesta vs term/definition)
- Theming uses inline hex values repeated 200+ times
- No caching, no error boundaries, 50+ `: any` types

## Detection

### 1. Content-Exists-but-Not-Rendering Audit

```bash
# For each page config, check if the content key actually resolves
cd /root/project
for f in nexa-pages/*.json; do
  slug=$(python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('$f')); print(d.get('slug','unknown'))")
  python3 -c "
import json
d_content = json.load(open('content/es.json'))
d_config = json.load(open('$f'))
for sec in d_config.get('sections',[]):
    content_key = sec.get('content','')
    if content_key:
        parts = content_key.split('.')
        obj = d_content
        for p in parts:
            obj = obj.get(p, {}) if isinstance(obj, dict) else None
        status = 'OK' if obj else 'MISSING'
        print(f'{sec[\"id\"]:20s} → {content_key:30s} [{status}]')
  " 2>/dev/null
done
```

### 2. Component-to-Section Mapping Gap Analysis

```bash
# Extract all section IDs from page configs
grep -roh '"id": "[^"]*"' nexa-pages/*.json | sort -u | while read line; do
  id=$(echo "$line" | cut -d'"' -f4)
  if ! grep -q "'$id'" src/pages/\\[slug\\].tsx src/pages/index.tsx 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "MISSING COMPONENT: $id"
  fi
done
```

## Core Architecture Pattern

### Three-tier rendering pipeline:

```
Page Config (nexa-pages/*.json)
  → SECTION_MAP (src/pages/[slug].tsx)
    → Section Component (src/components/sections*.tsx)
      → Content JSON (content/*.json, images.json)
```

### Section Component Contract

Every section component should accept these props:
```tsx
interface SectionComponentProps {
  variant?: string
  pageContent: Record<string, any>  // reconstructed as {keyName: data}
  data?: any        // the resolved section data (preferred)
  images?: any      // images.json manifest
  [key: string]: any
}
```

Component pattern:
```tsx
export function MySection({ pageContent, data, images }: SectionComponentProps) {
  const d = data || pageContent || {}  // data is preferred
  const items = d.items || []
  if (!items.length) return null       // early return if empty
  return (<section>...</section>)
}
```

### Section Dispatch (in page file) — CRITICAL PAGE CONTEXT FIX

The dispatch must reconstruct `pageContent` so that components looking up by content key name get the correct data. **Do NOT pass `sectionData || content` as pageContent directly** — section components look up `pageContent.hero`, not `pageContent` as the hero data itself.

**The one-line bug that silences all content:**
```tsx
// BROKEN — pageContent = the resolved sub-object (e.g. content.home.hero)
// But HeroSection does pageContent.hero which is content.home.hero.hero = null
<Comp pageContent={sectionData || content} />
```

**Two valid fixes:**

*Fix A — Use `pageContent` as the parent object:*
If the component expects `pageContent.hero` (most do), set `content: "home"` in the page config section, then `pageContent = content.home`. The component's `pageContent.hero` resolves to `content.home.hero` ✅.

*Fix B — Reconstruct pageContent:*
```tsx
const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id
const pc: any = {}
pc[keyName] = sectionData || content
<Comp pageContent={pc} data={sectionData} />
```
This wraps the section data in an object with the key name the component expects. Component does `pageContent.hero` → `pc.hero` → `sectionData` ✅.

**Key rule:** Components that accept both `pageContent` and `data` props:
- `pageContent` → the parent object that the component scans with `pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.trust`, etc.
- `data` → the specifically resolved sub-section data for components that use it directly (FeedbackSection, GenericSection)

```tsx
const Component = SECTION_MAP[section.id]
if (Component) {
  const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id)
  const pc: any = {}
  const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id
  pc[keyName] = sectionData || content
  return <ErrorBoundary key={key} name={section.id}>
    <Component pageContent={pc} data={sectionData} images={images} />
  </ErrorBoundary>
}
```

The `keyName` extracts the last segment of the content key (e.g. `home.hero` → `hero`, `faqPage.full` → `full`) which matches what the component looks up.

This pattern must be applied in BOTH `[slug].tsx` AND `index.tsx` — they share the same dispatch logic but are separate page files.

### Generic Fallback Renderer

For sections without dedicated components, render content generically:
- `data.items || data.full?.items || data.groups || data.pillars || data.members || data.paragraphs || data.trust?.items`
- `data.body || data.content`
- Headline, subheadline, eyebrow, CTA button
- Image resolution for memberImage and imageUrl

### Content Duplication Detection

When a dedicated page (e.g. `/servicios`) renders the same service/program content as the homepage, the issue is usually **content data duplication** in the JSON files, not in the rendering logic:

1. `home.services` and `servicesPage.services` both define the same 3 groups and 9 services
2. The page configs reference different content paths (`home.services` on homepage, `servicesPage.services` on servicios page)
3. Each resolves correctly, but the content under those paths is structurally identical

**Detection:**

```bash
# Compare two content paths from the JSON
python3 -c "
import json
d = json.load(open('content/en.json'))
h = d.get('home',{}).get('services',{})
s = d.get('servicesPage',{}).get('services',{})
h_groups = [g['title'] for g in h.get('groups',[])]
s_groups = [g['title'] for g in s.get('groups',[])]
print('home.services groups:', h_groups)
print('servicesPage.services groups:', s_groups)
print('SAME:', h_groups == s_groups)
"
```

**Full cross-page scan for all section types:**

```python
def content_signature(obj):
    \"\"\"Create a compact structural signature for comparison.\"\"\"
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        keys = sorted(k for k in obj.keys() if k not in ('seo', 'ctaHref', 'image', 'imageUrl', 'icon'))
        return tuple((k, content_signature(obj[k])) for k in keys)
    elif isinstance(obj, list):
        return tuple(content_signature(o) for o in obj[:3])
    return str(obj)[:60]

# Check two content paths for identical structure
h_sig = content_signature(d['home']['services'])
s_sig = content_signature(d['servicesPage']['services'])
print('IDENTICAL:', h_sig == s_sig)  # True means the data is the same under different paths
```

Also scan for **same-component intersections**: which section IDs appear across multiple pages, and which of those have identical content signatures.

```python
section_usage = {}
for page_id, config in all_pages.items():
    for sec in config.get('sections', []):
        sid = sec.get('id')
        if sid not in section_usage: section_usage[sid] = []
        section_usage[sid].append(page_id)
# Then filter to len >= 2
```

**Fix options:**
- **Option A: Remove from one page** — Remove the section from the homepage; the dedicated page covers it with more detail
- **Option B: Differentiate content** — Make the homepage version show fundamentally different content (icons/benefits summary vs detailed ✓ lists), using the same keys but shorter descriptions
- **Option C: Component-level dedup** — If the same component renders from different content paths but the data is identical, have the dedicated page config reference the homepage's content path directly

### Dedicated section types to always check for duplication

| Section ID | Typical duplicate pattern | Fix preference |
|---|---|---|
| `services` / `services-detail` | Homepage 9-card grid vs /servicios detailed page | Remove from homepage |
| `process-timeline` | Homepage 5-step timeline vs /proceso | Remove from homepage or replace with teaser |
| `programs-comparison` | Homepage 3-4 tier cards vs /programas | Remove from homepage |
| `cta-banner` | Same "Take the first step" CTA across 6+ pages | Make each page's CTA text unique |
| `trust-signals` | Same "One program/One trip" across 3+ pages | Differentiate content per page |
| `story` | Same "Our Story" on /sobre and /fundador | Give founder a personal bio instead |
| `newsletter-signup` | Same form on /blog and /recursos | Acceptable — intentional redundancy |
| `why-destination` | Same 3 pillars on home and dedicated | Minor — usually different enough |

## Data Key Standardization

### Common content structures in Spanish JSON files

| Content Type | Keys (preferred) | Keys (fallback) |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| FAQ items | `q`, `a` | `pregunta`, `respuesta`, `question`, `answer` |
| Glossary | `term`, `definition` | `q`, `a`, `title`, `description` |
| Team members | `name`, `role`, `description`, `memberImage` | `image`, `imageUrl` |
| Blog posts | `slug`, `title`, `excerpt`, `date`, `image`, `body` | `coverImage`, `author` |
| Pillars | `title`, `description`, `imageUrl`, `bullets` | `icon` |
| Services | `title`, `description` (grouped in `groups[]`) | `icon`, `price` |
| Process steps | `number`, `title`, `description`, `duration`, `image` | `time` |
| Stats | `value`, `label` | `number`, `description` |
| Privacy items | `q`, `a` | `title`, `body` |
| Testimonials | `name`, `quote`, `rating`, `image` | `author`, `text` |
| Gallery | `images[{src, alt, caption}]` | `items` |

### Standardize to prefer `q`/`a` for FAQ-like content

Spanish content files consistently use `q` and `a` as keys (shorter JSON = smaller bundle). Component fallback should check these first:

```tsx
const question = item.q || item.pregunta || item.question || item.title
const answer = item.a || item.respuesta || item.answer || item.description || item.body
```

```

grep -o '#[0-9A-Fa-f]\\{6\\}' src/components/*.tsx | sort -u
```
